content.problemStatement:
You are given a string SSS of length NNN consisting only of lowercase English letters.

A string is called an **odd string** if and only if:



For every pair of indices i<ji < ji<j
such that Si=SjS_i = S_jSi​=Sj​, the distance between them is odd, i.e.
(j−i)(j - i)(j−i) **is odd**.




In other words, no two equal characters are allowed to appear at an even distance
from each other.



You are allowed to rearrange the characters of the string SSS in any order.

Determine whether it is possible to rearrange SSS such that it becomes an odd string.


Input Format

The first line contains an integer TTT — the number of test cases.
For each test case:

The first line contains a single integer NNN — the length of the string.
The next line contains a string SSS consisting consisting of lowercase English letters.




Output Format
For each test case, output `YES` if it is possible to rearrange SSS into an odd
string, otherwise output `NO`.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `no`, `No`, and `nO` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: SSS consists of lowercase English letters only

sampleTestCases.input:
8
1
a
2
bb
5
xxxxx
7
aabccbd
8
aabccbdc
8
abcdabcd
8
codechef
5
abcde
sampleTestCases.output:
YES
YES
NO
YES
NO
YES
YES
YES
sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
For the first test case, a is already an odd string. So, no rearrangement is needed.


For the second test case, bb is already an odd string. b occurs at two indices 111 and 222. We can see that 2−1=12-1 = 12−1=1 is odd.


For the third test case, xxxxx remains unchanged however we rearrange. Now x occurs at indices 111 and 333. Since 3−1=23-1 = 23−1=2 is even, it is impossible to create an odd string.


For the fourth test case, aabccbd is already an odd string.


For the sixth test case, abcdabcd can be rearranged to aabbccdd which is an odd string.
